properties.name: The narrator's father’s family name is Pirrip
The narrator's Christian name is Philip
The narrator's infant tongue could make of both names nothing longer or more explicit than Pip
The narrator called himself Pip
The narrator came to be called Pip
The narrator gives Pirrip as his father's family name
The narrator states that his father is buried in a tombstone
The narrator states that his sister is Mrs. Joe Gargery
The narrator mentions that he never saw his father or mother
The narrator states that he never saw any likeness of his parents
The narrator reflects on his first fancies regarding his parents
The narrator derives ideas about his parents from their tombstones
The narrator has an odd idea about his father based on the tombstone letters
The narrator imagines his father as a square, stout, dark man with curly black hair
The narrator drew a childish conclusion about their mother.
The narrator perceived the inscription mentioning Georgiana.
The narrator felt that their mother was freckled and sickly.
Five little stone lozenges were arranged in a neat row beside their grave
The lozenges were sacred to the memory of five little brothers
The narrator expresses indebtedness for a belief
The narrator entertained a belief about their brothers
The narrator believed that their brothers had been born on their backs
The narrator believed that their brothers had their hands in their trousers-pockets
The narrator believed that their brothers never took their hands out in this state of existence
The narrator describes the marsh country by the river.
The river winds towards the sea.
The narrator reflects on their first vivid impression.
The narrator describes the afternoon as memorable and raw.
The narrator found out for certain that the place was the churchyard
Philip Pirrip was dead and buried
Georgiana, wife of Philip Pirrip, was dead and buried
Alexander, Bartholomew, Abraham, Tobias, and Roger were dead and buried
The narrator perceived the dark flat wilderness beyond the churchyard
The narrator perceived the marshes intersected with dikes and mounds and gates
The narrator perceived scattered cattle feeding on the marshes
The narrator perceived the low leaden line beyond as the river
The narrator perceived the distant savage lair from which the wind was rushing as the sea
Pip was growing afraid of it all and beginning to cry
A man started up from among the graves
A man cried a terrible voice
The man threatened to cut the throat of the little devil
The man expressed anger
The little devil was threatened
A man appeared, all in coarse grey, with a great iron on his leg.
The man had no hat.
The man wore broken shoes.
The man had an old rag tied round his head.
The man had been soaked in water.
The man had been smothered in mud.
The man had been lamed by stones.
The man had been cut by flints.
The man had been stung by nettles.
The man had been torn by briars.
The man limped.
The man shivered.
The man glared.
The man growled.
The man's teeth chattered in his head.
The man seized me by the chin.
The speaker pleaded in terror
The speaker asked not to have their throat cut
The man stared at the narrator
The man spoke the words 'Give it mouth!'
The man asked to be shown where someone lives
The man instructed to point out the place
I pointed to where our village lay
Our village lay on the flat in-shore
The village is a mile or more from the church
The man looked at me for a moment
The man turned me upside down
The man emptied my pockets
There was nothing in my pockets but a piece of bread
The church came to itself
The man made the church go head over heels before me
I saw the steeple under my feet
I was seated on a high tombstone
I was trembling while he ate the bread ravenously
The man addressed Pip as 'young dog'
The man licked his lips
The man commented on Pip's fat cheeks
The narrator believes the subjects were fat
The narrator was undersized for their years
The narrator was not strong
The man threatened with a shake of his head
The man expressed a desire to eat
The man indicated he has half a mind to eat them
I earnestly expressed my hope that he wouldn’t
I held tighter to the tombstone
I wanted to keep myself upon the tombstone
I wanted to keep myself from crying
I felt the need to hold onto the tombstone
The man asked where Pip's mother was
He started
He made a short run
He stopped
He looked over his shoulder
The speaker timidly explained something
The speaker identified Georgiana as their mother
He exclaimed 'Oh!'
He came back.
He asked if that was his father along with his mother.
The speaker acknowledges the presence of another person.
The speaker refers to the other person as 'late of this parish.'
He muttered
He considered
He questioned who the other person lives with
He expressed uncertainty about the other person's right to live
He stated he hasn't made up his mind
The narrator introduces his sister, Mrs. Joe Gargery.
The narrator identifies Joe Gargery as the blacksmith.
He said 'Blacksmith, eh?'
He looked down at his leg.
The man looked darkly at his leg and the narrator several times
The man came closer to the tombstone
The man took the narrator by both arms
The man tilted the narrator back as far as he could hold him
The man's eyes looked powerfully down into the narrator's
The narrator's eyes looked helplessly up into the man's
He said, 'the question being whether you’re to be let to live.'
He asked if the listener knows what a file is.
The listener feels threatened by the speaker's question.
He tilted me over a little more
I felt a greater sense of helplessness
I felt a sense of danger
The character demands a file and food.
The character threatens to take the heart and liver.
The character tilts the other character repeatedly.
The narrator felt dreadfully frightened
The narrator felt giddy
The narrator clung to him with both hands
The narrator asked him to let her keep upright
The narrator expressed hope that she wouldn't be sick
The narrator expressed hope that she could attend more
He gave me a most tremendous dip and roll
the church jumped over its own weathercock
He held me by the arms
I was in an upright position on the top of the stone
He went on in these fearful terms
The convict demands the file and food from Pip
The convict threatens Pip with violence if he fails to comply
The convict reveals he is not alone
The convict compares himself to a young man who is with him
The convict expresses that he is an angel compared to the young man
Pip feels fear in response to the convict's threats
A boy attempts to hide from that young man.
The boy locks his door.
The boy is warm in bed.
The boy tucks himself up.
The boy draws the clothes over his head.
The boy thinks himself comfortable and safe.
That young man softly creeps towards the boy.
That young man tears the boy open.
The narrator is keeping that young man from harming the boy.
The narrator finds it very hard to hold that young man off.
The narrator expresses difficulty in keeping that young man away from the boy.
The narrator asks the boy what he says.
I said that I would get him the file
I would get him what broken bits of food I could
I would come to him at the Battery
The man said 'Say Lord strike you dead if you don’t!'
He pursued the conversation
He reminded the listener of their undertaking
He reminded the listener of the young man
He instructed the listener to get home
He glanced about him over the cold wet flat
He expressed a wish to be a frog
He expressed a wish to be an eel
He hugged his shuddering body in both his arms
He limped towards the low church wall
I saw him go, picking his way among the nettles and brambles
He looked as if he were eluding the hands of the dead people
The dead people were stretching up cautiously out of their graves
He was picking his way among the nettles and brambles
He came to the low church wall
He got over the low church wall
He turned round to look for me
I set my face towards home
I made the best use of my legs
I looked over my shoulder
I saw him going on again towards the river
He was still hugging himself in both arms
He was picking his way with his sore feet among the great stones
The narrator stopped to look after him
The marshes appeared as a long black horizontal line
The river appeared as another horizontal line
The sky appeared as a row of long angry red lines and dense black lines intermixed
The narrator could faintly make out the only two black things in the prospect
The narrator observed the beacon
The narrator described the beacon as an ugly thing when near it
The narrator observed the gibbet with chains hanging to it
The man was limping towards the gibbet
The narrator implied the man was the pirate come to life
The man was going back to hook himself up again
The narrator felt a terrible turn
The narrator saw the cattle lifting their heads to gaze after him
The narrator wondered whether the cattle thought so too
The narrator looked all round for the horrible young man
The narrator could see no signs of him
The narrator felt frightened again
The narrator ran home without stopping
